about.title: About Us
about.subtitle: Car rental in Ulcinj with Podgorica Airport pickup, by locals who know the Bojana road, the kite beaches, and which fish restaurant to book first.
about.seoDesc: Ulcinj rentals built for the long stay: weekly and monthly rates, insurance bundled in, Podgorica Airport collection, and support for travellers basing on the southern coast.
about.p1: Car Rental Ulcinj connects travellers with reliable rental cars on Montenegro's southern coast. We work with trusted local operators to offer a range of vehicles, from compact city cars for Stari Grad's cobbles to SUVs for the sand tracks out to Ada Bojana.
about.heading2: Why a Car Makes Sense in Ulcinj
about.p2: Buses from Ulcinj run to Bar and Podgorica but nowhere near as often as you'd want. Velika Plaža is 12 km of flat sand, Ada Bojana sits at the river mouth, Valdanos hides in an olive grove, and the Sukobin border crossing into Albania is 20 minutes south. A rental car turns Ulcinj from a beach-week into a proper southern-coast, cross-border exploration.
about.p3a: We operate car rental services across
about.p3d: , and locations throughout the coast and interior.
about.promise1: Comprehensive cover with zero-excess upgrade available
about.promise2: Podgorica Airport meet-and-greet or Ulcinj door delivery, your car waits where you need it
about.promise3: Transparent pricing, the quoted figure is the final figure
about.promise4: Cross-border travel to Albania, Kosovo, Bosnia, Serbia, and Croatia with Green Card arranged
about.localText: Based in Ulcinj, we know every stretch of road along the southern coast, the best routes, the overlooked detours, and the parking tricks that guidebooks never mention. Whether you are heading from
about.localMid2: medieval Stari Grad, cruising the 12 km of
about.localEnd: , or crossing the Bojana to a fish restaurant on stilts at Ada Bojana, we can help you map out the ideal route.
about.contactText: Questions about renting a car in Ulcinj? We are available by phone, WhatsApp, and Viber. Browse the fleet online and reserve in minutes, or drop us a message and we will help you find the right vehicle for your trip.

fullInsurance.desc: Every rental includes CDW and third-party liability. Upgrade to zero excess if Stari Grad's cobbles or the unpaved spur to Ada Bojana make you nervous.

crossBorder.desc: Croatia via Debeli Brijeg, Bosnia through Trebinje, Albania from Ulcinj, Green Card paperwork prepared at booking time.

0.text: Flew into Podgorica (TGD) and picked the car up right at arrivals. Seventy kilometres down through the Sozina tunnel and we were on Velika Plaža before sunset, feet in the Adriatic. Three days of kitesurfing at Copacabana, a half-day run to Shkodër for lunch, and the car never missed a beat. Paperwork for the Sukobin border was tucked in the glovebox when we collected it.

1.text: Second year running we've rented with this crew. This time we based ourselves in the Stari Grad and spent a full week driving the southern coast — Ada Bojana for the fish stilts, Valdanos for the olive-grove cove, Lake Skadar via Virpazar. Having your own wheels out of Ulcinj means you can leave for Bar at six in the morning before the beach traffic builds. No fuss, no hidden fees.

2.text: Our Airbnb was on the hill above Mala Plaža and the rep met us there with the keys — saved us a taxi from TGD. We did the Ada Bojana river road, a day trip across the Sukobin crossing into Shkodër, and a long loop up to Podgorica and back along the Bar coast. Four adults, plenty of boot space, and the whole week came in cheaper than a single airport-to-Ulcinj transfer quote.

0.q: What do I need to bring for pickup?
0.a: A valid driving licence, your passport or national ID, and a physical credit card in the lead driver's name. Licences without Latin characters also require an International Driving Permit.

1.q: Can I cross into Croatia or other countries?
1.a: Absolutely. Albania is fifteen minutes via the Sukobin/Muriqan crossing south of Ulcinj — we include the Green Card at booking. Croatia is about three hours north via Debeli Brijeg, Kosovo and Serbia are also available with advance notice.

2.q: What age do I need to be?
2.a: The minimum is 21 years old with at least two years of driving experience. Drivers under 25 pay a young-driver supplement, the exact amount depends on the vehicle category.

3.q: What does the rental price cover?
3.a: Every booking includes CDW insurance, third-party liability, VAT, and a roadside safety kit. Most vehicles offer unlimited mileage, check the individual listing for details.

4.q: How do I collect the car at Podgorica Airport?
4.a: A representative meets you inside the Podgorica Airport arrivals hall with a name board. Your car is in the terminal car park, steps away. The drive south to Ulcinj via the Sozina tunnel and Bar takes around seventy minutes.

5.a: Yes — all rentals are round-trip. Collect and return at the same pickup point. One-way drop-offs are not currently offered; plan your route to loop back to the pickup location.

6.q: What if I need to cancel?
6.a: Most vehicles offer free cancellation up to seven days before pickup. Premium and convertible classes require thirty days. A small processing fee applies to all cancellations.

7.a: Most cars require between €100 and €350 held on a credit card. Some economy vehicles are deposit-free, these are clearly marked in your search results.

8.a: Many vehicles include unlimited mileage. Those with limits charge a per-kilometre rate beyond the allowance, always visible before you confirm.

9.a: None. The displayed price includes every tax and fee. No airport surcharges, no fuel-policy add-ons, nothing extra at the counter.

10.q: How much is the booking deposit?
10.a: A 15-20% advance payment secures your reservation. The balance is settled directly with the rental agent when you arrive to collect the vehicle.

11.q: Can I rent without a credit card?
11.a: A handful of vehicles are available without a credit card requirement. Look for the no-deposit filter when searching by pickup location.

aboutBody.h1: Rental Cars Matched to Ulcinj and the Southern Coast
aboutBody.p1: Car Rental Ulcinj partners with trusted local operators to put together a fleet suited to the southern Montenegrin coast: compact hatchbacks for the tight lanes inside Stari Grad, SUVs for the rutted track down to Valdanos and the gravel spurs above Velika Plaža, and diesel saloons for longer runs north to Podgorica or across the Sukobin border into Albania. Every car is supplied by established operators in Ulcinj and Bar, serviced regularly, and delivered with full insurance.
aboutBody.h2: Why a Car Changes Everything Here
aboutBody.p2: Scheduled buses follow the Ulcinj–Bar–Podgorica highway and stop. That is the extent of public transport. They will not detour to the fish-stilt restaurants at the mouth of the Bojana, or drop you beside a kite school on Velika Plaža, or navigate the single-track road that drops through the olive groves to Valdanos Bay. Only your own wheels and a willingness to explore unlock the south of Montenegro properly.
aboutBody.p3: We offer collection across the southern coast: Podgorica Airport (TGD, 70 km north via the Sozina tunnel), directly outside your Stari Grad apartment, beside Mala Plaža, at the Bar ferry terminal, and at 25+ further handover points. Economy hatchbacks thread through the Old Town approach lanes effortlessly. SUVs handle the loose gravel on the Ada Bojana track without breaking stride. Every vehicle carries full insurance, and cross-border documentation for Albania, Croatia, Bosnia, Serbia, and Kosovo is arranged at the time of booking.
aboutBody.promise1: Full insurance with a zero-excess upgrade on every reservation
aboutBody.promise2: A named representative at Podgorica Airport arrivals, sign in hand, vehicle parked outside
aboutBody.promise3: Transparent pricing, no airport levies, no fuel-policy surprises
aboutBody.promise4: Green Card paperwork arranged for five neighbouring countries
aboutBody.promise5: Free cancellation up to seven days before your collection date
aboutBody.promise6: Round-the-clock roadside support, from Ulcinj to Žabljak and everywhere in between
aboutBody.p4: Ask us which afternoons the Velika Plaža car parks fill with day-trippers from Shkodër, and we can point you toward the quieter Copacabana end of the beach, or suggest a loop inland through the olive groves instead. Ask about the unmarked turn-off for Valdanos Bay and we can draw the map for you. From Sukobin border queue patterns to the best hour to catch the Virpazar boatmen at Lake Skadar, we draw on local knowledge from partners and drivers working across the south.
aboutBody.p5: WhatsApp, Viber, a traditional phone call, whichever suits you. We respond quickly, even during the frantic August beach weeks. Browse the fleet online, lock in a rate, and we take care of the rest. Or message us a question about parking near Mala Plaža, the best route to Shkodër, or whether the Ada Bojana bridge is passable after heavy rain.
aboutBody.h6: Our Vehicles and What They Handle
aboutBody.p6: Each car in the fleet is chosen for the realities of southern Montenegrin roads. The compact hatchbacks — Fiat Panda, VW Polo, Renault Clio — slot into the narrow lanes inside Stari Grad where larger vehicles simply cannot fit. For families or longer trips, the mid-size SUVs (Dacia Duster, Nissan Qashqai) carry luggage comfortably and handle the loose gravel on the Ada Bojana approach and the hairpins up toward Rumija without hesitation. Diesel saloons suit the motorway run to Podgorica or the cross-border highway to Shkodër. Vehicles are serviced by our partner operators in Ulcinj and Bar, checked between rentals, and delivered with a full tank and verified tyre pressures.
aboutBody.h7: A Typical Week Behind the Wheel
aboutBody.p7: Monday morning: collect at Podgorica Airport, drive south through the Sozina tunnel to Ulcinj, spend the afternoon walking the pirate ramparts of Stari Grad. Tuesday: straight down the coastal road to Velika Plaža for a kitesurfing lesson at Copacabana. Wednesday: the Ada Bojana river loop for fish lunch on a stilted terrace. Thursday: cross at Sukobin and spend the afternoon in Shkodër at Rozafa Castle and the Marubi photography museum. Friday: the winding back roads up to Lake Skadar via Virpazar, a boat trip across the lily pads, Crmnica-valley wine on the way home. Saturday: Valdanos Bay in the morning for a swim beneath the olive trees, Bar Old Town in the afternoon. Sunday: drop off in Ulcinj. That circuit covers roughly 400 km. Fuel: around 40 to 50 euros at current diesel prices. No bus, tour group, or taxi could replicate it at any price.
aboutBody.p8: Summer brings the beach crowds, with Velika Plaža reaching peak occupancy from mid-July through late August and Shkodër-side Albanians flooding in for weekends. We advise arriving at the beach before 10:00 or parking at the Copacabana end and walking. Winter transforms Ulcinj into a quieter place: fewer visitors, lower rates, and mountain roads over Rumija that occasionally close above 1,000 metres. Spring and autumn are the sweet spot. April wildflowers line the Valdanos track, September still offers warm seas without the crowds, and the Ada Bojana road is open and uncrowded.

montenegroBody.h1: Driving Montenegro from an Ulcinj Base
montenegroBody.p1: Montenegro is roughly the size of Northern Ireland yet contains a coast, a deep fjord-like bay, a 2,500-year-old walled town, a 1,300-metre-deep canyon, and alpine peaks above 2,500 metres. Driving distances look short on a map. In practice, the roads twist, climb, tunnel, and ferry-cross their way between these extremes, and journey times rarely match what GPS predicts. Accept that, and the driving itself becomes part of the holiday.
montenegroBody.p2: Ulcinj sits at the southern tip of the country, which is less of a handicap than the map suggests. Bar is thirty minutes north. Podgorica Airport is seventy minutes via the Sozina tunnel. Shkodër in Albania is forty-five minutes through the Sukobin crossing. Lake Skadar is an hour inland. Only the Bay of Kotor in the far north-west and Durmitor in the far north require a genuine road trip. Everything else radiates outward from Stari Grad like spokes from a hub.
montenegroBody.startText: Podgorica Airport (TGD), 70 km north of Ulcinj, is the obvious collection point, around seventy minutes from landing to Stari Grad via the Sozina tunnel and Bar. Tirana Airport in Albania offers budget routes and connects through the Sukobin crossing in roughly 2.5 hours. Dubrovnik Airport in Croatia has the most international routes but sits a full four hours north via two border crossings. All three have meet-and-greet handovers.
montenegroBody.timesText1: Velika Plaža: 10 min. Ada Bojana: 20 min. Bar: 30 min. Sukobin border: 15 min. Shkodër (Albania): 45 min. Virpazar (Lake Skadar): 60 min. Podgorica Airport: 70 min. Dubrovnik: 4 h. Žabljak (Durmitor): 4.5 h.
montenegroBody.timesText2: The old mountain road from Virpazar over Rumija back down to the coast contains tight hairpin bends, narrow passing places, and no guard rails in long stretches. Navigation apps routinely underestimate mountain stretches by 20-30%. Plan accordingly and carry water.
montenegroBody.rulesIntro: Random roadside checks are common, especially on the coastal road south of Bar and near the Sukobin and Debeli Brijeg border crossings. Officers will ask for:
montenegroBody.rule1: A valid driving licence (international driving permit accepted alongside it)
montenegroBody.rule2: The original rental contract (photocopies are not sufficient)
montenegroBody.rule3: Proof of insurance (the rental company provides this in the vehicle)
montenegroBody.rule4: A Green Card if crossing any border (approximately 15 euros for 15 days)
montenegroBody.keyRule1: Seat belts: mandatory for every occupant, front and rear
montenegroBody.keyRule2: Mobile phones: hands-free only, and even then police may stop you
montenegroBody.keyRule3: Alcohol: zero-tolerance policy, any detectable blood alcohol means an on-the-spot fine or worse
montenegroBody.keyRule4: Speed cameras: fixed units on the Ulcinj–Bar coastal road and mobile traps on the Sozina motorway. Fines are issued to the rental company and charged back to your card
montenegroBody.conditionsTitle: What the Roads Are Actually Like
montenegroBody.conditionsText: The coast road between Ulcinj and Bar is fully paved and maintained, two lanes almost throughout, with a handful of tighter sections around Sutomore. Mountain roads above 800 metres across Rumija may lack guard rails entirely. The Sozina tunnel between Bar and Podgorica is modern and well-lit. The inland road to Virpazar and Lake Skadar is narrow, twisty, and has limited passing places — honk before blind bends. In winter, chains or winter tyres are legally required above the snow line.
montenegroBody.e65Text: This route traces the entire Montenegrin coast from Ulcinj and Bar north through the central coast toward Herceg Novi, then across the Debeli Brijeg border into Croatia. The Sozina tunnel shortcuts the Bar–Podgorica leg by 40 minutes. The coast road is where most visitors spend their driving time, and for good reason — the views change with every headland.
montenegroBody.e762Text: Cuts inland from the Adriatic through the Montenegrin heartland toward the Serbian and eastern Bosnian borders. This is the route to Niksic, the Piva canyon, and eventually Durmitor. Less scenic near the coast but increasingly dramatic as it climbs.
montenegroBody.borderText: Cross-border driving is permitted with a Green Card. The Croatia crossing at Debeli Brijeg is the busiest, in July and August, waits of one to two hours are normal. Weekday mornings before 08:00 or evenings after 20:00 cut the queue significantly. Other crossings (Albania, Bosnia, Serbia, Kosovo) are generally faster.
montenegroBody.nationText: Independent only since 2006, Montenegro remains lightly visited compared to Croatia or Greece. The roads are quieter, the parking easier, and the landscapes more concentrated. A single tank of fuel can take you from Ulcinj’s Ottoman-era ramparts to a glacial mountain lake and back. Few countries this small pack this much into their borders.
montenegroBody.fuelP1: Montenegro has three main fuel brands: Jugopetrol (part of Hellenic Petroleum), Eko, and several independent stations. Prices are regulated and relatively uniform across the country at approximately 1.45 to 1.55 euros per litre for diesel and slightly more for petrol. All major stations accept credit cards, though smaller independent stations in the north occasionally require cash.
montenegroBody.fuelP2: On the coast, fuel stations are frequent, you will pass one every 15 to 20 km along the main highways. In the mountains, they thin out considerably. Fill your tank in Ulcinj or Bar before heading inland toward Virpazar, Cetinje, or anywhere in the interior. The mountain road over Rumija and the old road between Cetinje and Podgorica have no fuel stops at all.
montenegroBody.speedP1: Built-up areas: 50 km/h. Open roads: 80 km/h. Dual carriageways and motorway-standard roads: 100 km/h (or 130 km/h where posted). The coast road through Ulcinj, Sutomore, and Bar fluctuates between 40 and 60 km/h depending on the settlement. Speed cameras are fixed at several points along the Ulcinj–Bar stretch and at the entrance to the Sozina tunnel.
montenegroBody.speedP2: Mobile police speed traps appear randomly, particularly on the Sozina motorway between Bar and Podgorica and on the approach to border crossings. Fines are issued on the spot and range from 30 to 200 euros depending on how far over the limit you were travelling. The rental company is notified electronically if a camera catches you, and the fine is charged to your credit card.
montenegroBody.mountainP1: Montenegrin mountain roads are beautiful and demanding in equal measure. The old Rumija road between Virpazar and the coast is narrow and steep, with unguarded drops on many sections. The road to Durmitor via the Piva canyon is 170 km of continuous mountain driving with narrow bridges, unlit tunnels, and occasional rockfall debris.
montenegroBody.mountainP2: Practical advice: use low gears on descents to save your brakes. Honk before blind bends, it is standard practice and potentially lifesaving. Carry at least 2 litres of water per person. Keep headlights on even in daytime. Pull into passing places to let faster or larger vehicles through. And budget 30 percent more time than your GPS suggests.
montenegroBody.startLink: Start at {{link}}, the closest international airport to Ulcinj.

borderCrossingBody.h1: Taking a Rental Car Across Balkan Borders
borderCrossingBody.p1: Montenegro shares land borders with five countries: Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Albania, Kosovo, and Serbia. Driving a rental car into any of them is permitted, provided you inform the rental company at booking so the paperwork is ready when you collect the keys.
borderCrossingBody.greenCardText: A Green Card (Carte Internationale d'Assurance Automobile) is a standardised document proving your vehicle carries valid third-party insurance in the country you are entering. Border guards request it at every Balkan checkpoint. Without one, you will be turned around.
borderCrossingBody.greenCard1: Typical cost: around 15 euros for a 15-day period
borderCrossingBody.greenCard2: Issued by: the rental company, at no hassle to you, just confirm the countries you plan to visit when booking
borderCrossingBody.greenCard3: Standard coverage: Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia. Kosovo may need to be added separately
borderCrossingBody.greenCardWarn: Before setting off, open the glovebox and verify the Green Card lists every country on your route. A card valid for Croatia alone will not get you into Bosnia. Five minutes of checking saves hours of frustration at the checkpoint.
borderCrossingBody.doc1: Passport or EU national ID card (must be original, not a photocopy)
borderCrossingBody.doc2: Driving licence, an international driving permit is recommended alongside non-Latin-script licences
borderCrossingBody.doc3: Original rental contract with company stamp (printed, not a screenshot on your phone)
borderCrossingBody.doc4: Green Card insurance certificate listing all destination countries
borderCrossingBody.doc5: Vehicle registration certificate (kept in the car by the rental company)
borderCrossingBody.croatiaText: The E65 coastal crossing between Herceg Novi and Dubrovnik is by far the most popular, and therefore the most congested. In July and August, two passport checks (Montenegrin exit, Croatian entry) combine with heavy tourist traffic to produce queues of one to two hours. The crossing itself is a simple road checkpoint with booths on both sides.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaTip: Timing matters enormously. Cross before 08:00 or after 20:00 and the wait drops to minutes. Weekday mornings are dramatically quieter than Saturday or Sunday. If you must cross mid-afternoon in August, bring water and patience.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaFerry: Heading north from Ulcinj, the E65 runs up the coast past Bar and the Kamenari–Lepetane ferry across the mouth of the bay, which saves a 45-minute drive around the inner shore. Allow three hours to reach the Debeli Brijeg border crossing into Croatia.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaTitle: Into Albania: Sukobin or Hani i Hotit
borderCrossingBody.albaniaText: The coastal crossing at Sukobin/Muriqan, south of Ulcinj, is the more straightforward route, well-signed, with generally short queues. The inland alternative at Hani i Hotit, near the southern shore of Lake Skadar, is scenic but quieter and less familiar to foreign tourists.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaNote: Albanian road infrastructure is improving rapidly but remains unpredictable outside main highways. Speed limits drop on rural roads and surface quality can change without warning. Drive defensively, particularly at night.
borderCrossingBody.bosniaTitle: Into Bosnia: Scepan Polje or Vilusi
borderCrossingBody.bosniaText: Heading north from the coast, the Scepan Polje crossing near Foca accesses the Tara River gorge and Sutjeska National Park. From Niksic, the Vilusi crossing provides a route toward Trebinje and eventually Mostar. Both are lightly used and rarely involve waiting.
borderCrossingBody.serbiaText: The Dobrakovo checkpoint on the E65, north of Bijelo Polje, connects to the Serbian motorway network. From there, Belgrade is roughly four hours of straightforward motorway driving. Tolls are charged at motorway entry and exit booths, take a ticket when you join and pay based on distance when you leave.
borderCrossingBody.kosovoText: The Kula crossing near Rozaje leads to Peja/Pec in western Kosovo. Confirm with your rental company that Kosovo is explicitly listed on your Green Card, several insurers exclude it by default, and border guards will check.
borderCrossingBody.tip1: Arrange documents in a single envelope on the dashboard before joining the queue. Fumbling at the window wastes time
borderCrossingBody.tip2: Switch off the engine while stationary, guards notice and it puts them at ease
borderCrossingBody.tip3: Avoid the Debeli Brijeg crossing on Saturday and Sunday afternoons between June and September
borderCrossingBody.tip4: Inland crossings (Bosnia, Serbia, Kosovo) are almost always faster than the coastal Croatian one
borderCrossingBody.tip5: Carry euros in cash. Croatian motorway tolls accept cards, but Bosnian and Albanian fuel stations sometimes do not
borderCrossingBody.tip6: Check your mobile roaming plan before crossing. Data charges spike the moment your phone connects to a foreign network
borderCrossingBody.lawsTitle: Traffic Laws That Change at the Border
borderCrossingBody.lawsIntro: Regulations differ from one country to the next. The main surprises:
borderCrossingBody.law1: Croatia: daytime running lights mandatory at all times. Motorway tolls are collected by cash or card at barrier stations
borderCrossingBody.law2: Albania: lower speed limits on secondary roads than in Montenegro. Livestock on the carriageway is not uncommon outside towns
borderCrossingBody.law3: Bosnia: winter tyres compulsory from November through April. A first-aid kit and warning triangle must be in the vehicle
borderCrossingBody.law4: Serbia: absolute zero-tolerance alcohol limit. Motorways use toll booths, take a ticket on entry, pay on exit based on distance
borderCrossingBody.law5: Kosovo: right-hand traffic, same as Montenegro. An international driving permit is strongly recommended
borderCrossingBody.route1: Ulcinj to Shkodër: 45 minutes via the Sukobin/Muriqan crossing, one border, and the easiest cross-border day trip in Montenegro
borderCrossingBody.route2: Ulcinj to Dubrovnik: 4 hours up the coastal E65, one border at Debeli Brijeg. Arguably the finest long coastal drive in the Mediterranean
borderCrossingBody.route3: Ulcinj to Tirana: roughly 3 hours via Sukobin and the Albanian coastal highway. A different country, a different pace entirely
borderCrossingBody.route4: Ulcinj to Belgrade: 7-8 hours via Podgorica and the E65 north. Motorway most of the way on the Serbian side
borderCrossingBody.returnP: The return crossing is usually faster than the outbound one, especially at Debeli Brijeg. Montenegrin border officers are generally efficient and rarely hold up traffic. Make sure you still have all documents easily accessible, the Green Card, rental contract, and passport will be checked again. Refuel before crossing back into Montenegro if you have been driving in Croatia, as Croatian fuel prices tend to be slightly higher.
borderCrossingBody.ctaText: Looking for a car that comes with cross-border paperwork already sorted? Search our fleet, tick the countries you plan to visit at booking, and the Green Card will be in the glovebox when you collect the keys.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaFerryLink: Hiring from {{link}} is the most popular cross-border option.

blogIndex.heroSubtitle: Road routes, local secrets, and quiet corners of the southern Montenegrin coast, from people who drive these roads daily.
blogIndex.card_churches_title: Stari Grad: Ulcinj’s Old Town Pirate Fortress
blogIndex.card_churches_excerpt: A walk through the 2,500-year-old citadel that once housed Barbary corsairs, a Cervantes prison cell, and a slave-trade market. The ramparts, the cisterns, and the quietest corners after sundown.
blogIndex.card_vrmac_title: Ada Bojana: Fish Stilts and the FKK Beach
blogIndex.card_vrmac_excerpt: The river-mouth island where the Bojana meets the Adriatic. Where to park, which stilted konoba serves the best grilled sea bass, and how the naturist end of the beach actually works.
blogIndex.card_markets_title: Velika Plaža: Kite Schools and the Long Beach
blogIndex.card_markets_excerpt: Twelve kilometres of flat sand, shallow warm water, and reliable cross-shore wind. Which school to book with, where to park for free, and the quietest hour to arrive in August.
blogIndex.card_swimming_excerpt: Steps from Stari Grad, Ulcinj’s horseshoe of fine sand is busy in August but a bargain in June and September. Best konobas above the sand, parking hacks, and how the evening promenade works.
blogIndex.card_cetinje_title: Podgorica Airport to Ulcinj: The 70 km Drive
blogIndex.card_cetinje_excerpt: Step-by-step directions from TGD arrivals to Stari Grad via the Sozina tunnel and Bar, including fuel stops, speed-camera locations, and the best coffee break on the way down.
blogIndex.card_dobrota_title: Valdanos Bay: Olive Groves and a Hidden Cove
blogIndex.card_dobrota_excerpt: Turn off the coastal road and drop through a forest of centuries-old olive trees to one of Montenegro’s most sheltered swimming coves. Rough track, rocky entry, and almost no one there.
blogIndex.card_kayak_title: Skadar Lake from Ulcinj: The Virpazar Back Road
blogIndex.card_kayak_excerpt: How to string together a boat trip on the Balkans’ largest lake with a winery lunch in the Crmnica valley and a return down the Rumija mountain road. One full day, one tank of fuel.
blogIndex.card_stoliv_title: Ulcinj to Shkodër: The Sukobin Border Drive
blogIndex.card_stoliv_excerpt: Forty-five minutes, one border, and a completely different country on the other side. Which queue to join at Sukobin, what Albanian car insurance costs, and where to park in Shkodër.
blogIndex.card_wildflowers_excerpt: Rozafa Castle, the Marubi photography museum, and the Ottoman quarter. How to spend six hours in northern Albania’s most atmospheric city and still be back in Ulcinj for sunset.

blogHome.sectionSubtitle: Road routes, quiet corners, and local tips for exploring Ulcinj and the southern coast by car.

cookieBody.whatText: Cookies are small text files stored on your device when you visit a website. They help the site remember your preferences and understand how you use it.
cookieBody.essentialText: These are necessary for the website to function. They cannot be disabled.
cookieBody.sessionDesc: maintain your browsing session while you navigate the site
cookieBody.preferenceDesc: remember your language and location selections
cookieBody.thirdPartyText: Our car rental booking widget (provided by LocalRent) may set cookies to:
cookieBody.thirdParty2: Maintain your session during the booking process
cookieBody.thirdPartyNote: These cookies are set by LocalRent and are governed by their privacy policy.
cookieBody.managingText: You can control cookies through your browser settings. Most browsers allow you to:
cookieBody.managingNote: Note that blocking essential cookies may affect the functionality of the booking widget.
cookieBody.contactText: Questions about our use of cookies? Contact us at info@carrentalulcinj.com.

privacyBody.whoText: Car Rental Ulcinj operates as an affiliate partner connecting travellers with car rental providers in and around Ulcinj, Montenegro. Our website is located at carrentalulcinj.com. When you use our site, we may collect certain information as described below.
privacyBody.provideText: When you use our booking search form, we collect the pickup location, dates, and times you enter in order to find available vehicles. This information is passed to our partner (LocalRent) to generate search results. We do not collect or store payment details, all payments are processed directly by the rental provider.
privacyBody.auto1: pages visited, time spent on site, referring website
privacyBody.auto2: browser type, operating system, screen resolution
privacyBody.auto3: used for approximate location and security purposes
privacyBody.how1: To connect you with car rental availability and pricing
privacyBody.how2: To improve our website and user experience
privacyBody.how3: To understand how visitors use our site (analytics)
privacyBody.how4: To respond to enquiries via WhatsApp, Viber, or email
privacyBody.thirdText: Our website uses the following third-party services:
privacyBody.third1: car rental booking widget, processes your search queries and handles all booking and payment
privacyBody.thirdNote: Each of these services has their own privacy policy governing how they handle data.
privacyBody.cookiesText: We use minimal cookies necessary for the website to function. The LocalRent booking widget may set its own cookies for session management and preferences. See our {{link}} for details.
privacyBody.right1: Request access to any personal data we hold about you
privacyBody.right2: Request correction or deletion of your data
privacyBody.right4: Lodge a complaint with a data protection authority
privacyBody.contactText: For any privacy-related questions, contact us via email at info@carrentalulcinj.com.

termsBody.aboutText: Car Rental Ulcinj (carrentalulcinj.com) is an affiliate website that connects travellers with car rental services in and around Ulcinj, Montenegro. We are not a car rental company, we partner with LocalRent to provide real-time availability and booking services.
termsBody.bookingP1: All car rental bookings made through our website are processed and fulfilled by our partner, LocalRent. When you complete a booking, you enter into a rental agreement directly with the vehicle supplier, not with Car Rental Ulcinj.
termsBody.bookingP2: The rental terms, including cancellation policies, insurance coverage, age requirements, and payment conditions, are set by the vehicle supplier and displayed during the booking process. We recommend reading these terms carefully before confirming any booking.
termsBody.provide1: A search interface to browse available rental vehicles
termsBody.provide2: Destination and travel information about Ulcinj and the southern Montenegrin coast
termsBody.provide3: Customer support to help with enquiries before booking
termsBody.doNot1: Own, operate, or maintain any rental vehicles
termsBody.doNot3: Set rental prices, insurance terms, or cancellation policies
termsBody.pricingText: Prices displayed on our website are provided in real-time by our booking partner and are subject to change. The final price is confirmed at the time of booking. All prices include VAT and mandatory insurance unless otherwise stated.
termsBody.contentText: We make every effort to ensure that information on this website is accurate and up to date. However, travel information, driving regulations, and border crossing requirements can change. We recommend verifying critical details (especially border crossing documents and driving rules) with official sources before your trip.
termsBody.liabilityText: Car Rental Ulcinj acts as an intermediary and is not liable for:
termsBody.liability1: The condition or availability of rental vehicles
termsBody.liability2: Actions or omissions by rental suppliers
termsBody.liability3: Losses arising from border crossing issues or documentation problems
termsBody.liability4: Changes to prices after booking confirmation
termsBody.ipText: All content on this website, including text, design, and graphics, is owned by Car Rental Ulcinj unless otherwise credited. Stock photography is used under licence from Pexels.
termsBody.contactText: Questions about these terms? Contact us at info@carrentalulcinj.com.

popularRoutes.title: Scenic Drives from Ulcinj
popularRoutes.subtitle: Four roads worth renting a car for.

0.title: Ulcinj to Velika Plaža
0.desc: Ten minutes south on the Bojana road to 12 km of flat sand and the kite schools at Copacabana.

1.title: Ulcinj to Ada Bojana
1.desc: Along the river mouth to the fish-stilt restaurants and the naturist island at the Albanian border.

2.title: Ulcinj to Shkodër
2.desc: South through the Sukobin crossing into Albania — Rozafa Castle, Ottoman quarter, and lunch by the lake.

3.title: Ulcinj to Podgorica Airport
3.desc: North via the Sozina tunnel and Bar — the standard TGD handover route.

0.title: Kitesurf Season Clears the Fleet
0.text: Velika Plaža fills with kitesurfers from May to September — and the rental fleet thins with them. Lock in your car a couple of weeks before a July or August pickup.

1.title: Ada Bojana Sand Needs Four Wheels
1.text: Tarmac ends at the Ada Bojana bridge. The sand tracks to the fish restaurants and the river mouth chew tyres on two-wheel-drive cars — book a Jeep or Stonic if this is on your list.

2.title: Sukobin Is the Quiet Border
2.text: The Sukobin-Muriqan crossing to Albania sits 20 minutes south of town and rarely has more than two cars ahead of you. Albanian Green Card included at booking when you mention the Shkodër trip.

fleetIndex.title: Our Ulcinj Rental Fleet
fleetIndex.subtitle: Seven cars picked for a multi-day Ulcinj base, with day-trips out to Lake Skadar, Shkodër, Bar and across the Sukobin border.
fleetIndex.seoDesc: Seven multi-day rental cars for Ulcinj — mid-size wagons, diesel cruisers and a city Fiat. Specs, fuel range, and which car suits day-trips along the southern coast.
fleetIndex.intro1: This fleet is built around the multi-day Ulcinj renter — visitors who anchor themselves at a Stari Grad apartment or a Mala Plaža guesthouse for a fortnight or longer, and who expect to be spending real time on the road between beach days. That brief is different from a one-day hire or a pure TGD airport pickup: it leans on boot space for a weekly shop at Voli, diesel torque for the Sozina motorway run up to Podgorica, and a seating position that keeps four adults comfortable on the 4.5-hour push to Žabljak. The seven cars below are chosen accordingly.
fleetIndex.intro2: The mix is deliberately weighted toward mid-size. A Peugeot 308 with the 1.5 BlueHDi and the EAT auto, a Renault Megane diesel with the segment’s biggest boot, a VW Golf with the DSG 2.0 TDI — these are the cars that turn an Ulcinj → Virpazar → Lake Skadar → Sozina loop from a gear-changing chore into a cruise. For shorter hops we keep a Renault Clio, a Citroen C3 with Advanced Comfort dampers, a Kia Stonic crossover for the rutted spur roads down to Valdanos and up the Rumija switchbacks, and a tiny Fiat 500 for when Stari Grad parking is the deciding constraint. No one-trick supercars, no vans; every car here justifies a multi-day hire.
fleetIndex.intro3: A practical Ulcinj note before you choose. Diesel remains the smarter spec for anyone planning even one cross-border run — Shkodër via Sukobin is 45 minutes each way, Tirana is 2.5 hours, and Dubrovnik via Debeli Brijeg is a full day. The 308 and Megane will cover the Albanian trips comfortably on a single tank, the Golf is the most refined motorway car we have for the Sozina run, and the petrol Clio will still do an Ulcinj → Shkodër return on a quarter tank. If your week is coast-contained — Velika Plaža, Mala Plaža, Ada Bojana, Valdanos — the Fiat 500 or the C3 saves a third on weekly fuel and parks in places the rest of the fleet can’t. Pick on your itinerary, not the daily rate.
